hook.text: At 380,000 years old, the universe cooled to 3,000°C. Electrons finally combined with nuclei to form atoms.

buildup.text: Before this, photons bounced endlessly off free electrons. The universe was an opaque, glowing fog.

discovery.text: Once atoms formed, photons could travel freely. Light was released—the oldest light we can ever detect.

twist.text: That ancient light is still visible today as the Cosmic Microwave Background—stretched by expansion into microwaves.

climax.text: The CMB is a snapshot of the infant universe. Tiny hot and cold spots became the seeds of all cosmic structure.

punchline.text: The moment the universe became transparent. Light has traveled ever since.

quiz.question: What happened during the Recombination Era?
options: Electrons combined with nuclei, allowing light to travel freely for the first time
The first stars formed and began emitting visible light
Dark matter separated from normal matter
